gazing into its giant ocean tank and while you're here you can enjoy the Osaka Bay Santa Maria Cruise Legoland and giant temples on Ferris wheel it goes without saying that Kyoto is a great side trip from Osaka and I'll leave my four day guide to Kyoto pinned up here it's only 30 minutes away and you could take the Cure train garaku an artistically Japanese train with its own stone garden on board Nara and its deer Parks can also be reached in under an hour from Osaka which makes for a fantastic afternoon those of you on the Jr pass could also take a trip down to himeji castle at no additional expense but an often underrated city is Kobe Kobe is only 30 minutes away and besides its most famous attraction Kobe beef features its own Chinatown with a creative spin on the dish Kobe can absolutely not be missed if you're traveling in December during its illumination Festival which serves as an ongoing memorial for the Great hanshin Earthquake of 1995 with over 200 000 hand-painted harakas just to name a few which makes this pass a real money saver and great alternative during the IC card shortage although you cannot use it on the Jr lines you can buy these at the start of the day at most station Masters or for further discounts you could pick one up on clue which offers discount tickets for hundreds of locations around Japan including Universal Studios entry and express tickets so you won't miss out on getting in you can use the link in my description and the code inaka five off for a discount while also helping to support Channel cheers Osaka can be visited all year round and there's always something to do spring is a great time to visit Osaka Castle or the World Expo park for the thousands of blooming cherry blossoms and for many this is the most desired time to travel Yoshino in particular is just an hour away from Osaka by luxury sightseeing train and is considered Japan's top soccer spot with over 30 000 cherry trees covering the mountain summer in Osaka actually makes for a very good trip while I always caution people that Japanese summers are brutally hot and around 38 degrees Celsius with 100 humidity there are some amazing festivals that can be viewed at this time earlier in the video I mentioned tanjin Festival but you can also see the illuminations of the Okawa River for talabata in July or one of Japan's most intense festivals danjity Matsuri held in both September and October summer is also the time for fireworks in Japan and the yoragawa fireworks festival is one of Japan's largest which is held in August Autumn is my absolute favorite season in Japan not too hot and not too cold just right for out outdoor activities hiking and seeing the beautiful fall colors and you'll find no shortage of places to do this in Osaka it is also a lot less busy than spring which means travel will be cheaper and accommodation easier to find October will also offer the Halloween Horror Nights Universal Studios experience where zombies will chase you around the park and Halloween in Japan and especially Osaka is just nuts something that you have to see at least once as people flood the streets in costumes winter also makes for a fine videos.transcript: Osaka, a captivating city known for its blend of historical charm, lively spirit and culinary delights. I've had the chance to explore Osaka and its many offerings. The city stands as a bustling metropolis that beckons travelers to explore its vibrant streets and indulge in its famous food offerings. If you're planning to visit Osaka and wondering how to best approach this city, then let me share with you my guide to spending 4 days in Osaka. Osaka is located in the heart of Japan. With a population of 2.7 million, the city is a vital transportation and economic hub. To best explore the city, I recommend staying near the central area specifically near the famous Dotonbori, known for its neon-lit canal, the Glico man sign and being home to numerous restaurants and street food stands. It's also near the busy shopping district of Shinsaibashi, but more importantly, you'll have access to Namba Station, a major transportation hub in Osaka. You can also find the famous Kuromon Market, often referred to as the "Kitchen of Osaka". It's therefore very convenient staying near Dotonbori as you'll have easy access to the rest of the city via public transport and there are plenty of restaurants and entertainment options around the area. On my last trip to Osaka, I stayed at the Hotel The Flag in Shinsaibashi. It's a relatively new hotel that comes with modern and clean rooms with all the usual amenities but the best part about the hotel is its location. It's only a 3-minute walk from the hotel to Shinsaibashi Station and only a 10-minute walk to Dotonbori which is super convenient. There are many more nearby hotels you can stay at, but I'll leave links to hotel recommendations and more in the description box below. For transportation, trains and buses will be your main way of getting around the city. This is why getting your hands on an IC card is an absolute must. Either a physical card or you can add a digital version to your Apple wallet if you have an iPhone. Simply tap with your phone while entering a station and tap out when you exit. It's easy to keep track of your travel expenses and I found that using the digital card the most convenient as I could top up the card straight from the phone using a credit card. It may also be worth considering the Osaka Amazing Pass. It's a special sightseeing and transportation pass designed for tourists and covers access to various well-known attractions and includes unlimited use of trains and buses within the city. You can purchase the Osaka Amazing Pass at tourist information offices in Osaka or online via Klook which you may find more convenient. More info and links below. On our first day in Osaka, we'll wake up bright and early and start off our day by visiting the Namba Yasaka Shrine. The shrine is mostly known for its large lion head stage, which is said to swallow up any bad luck or spirits plaguing you. Most of the shrine's original structures were lost due to air raids and fires, so the buildings you'll encounter here are mostly reconstructions from the post-war era. As always, it's customary to clean your hands and pay your respects when visiting a shrine. Next, we head over to Shinsaibashi and walk through the main shopping street. You'll find hundreds of stores lining down its 600m long street. You'll find anything from small cute stores to larger department stores such as Uniqlo and the Parco Shopping Complex. It's here that we stumble across this peculiar shop called Fueki which sells a diversity of items featuring Fueki-kun the official mascot of the brand. We decided to opt for the doll head soft serve offering. Just your standard vanilla soft serve. Nothing special, but you get to keep the creepy doll head though. Nice souvenir. From Shinsaibashi, we make the short walk over to Amerika-mura, also known as the American Village. It's a lively and trendy district known for its vibrant atmosphere, fashion-forward shops, and unique blend of American and Japanese pop culture. Amerika-mura is especially popular among the youth. You can find plenty of clothing stores, thrifting stores, and vintage boutiques catering to different tastes. We then head over to Umeda and make a quick stop at the Pokemon Center. If you're a fan of Pokemon, then this is a store that shouldn't be missed. The store is an official Pokemon store and offers a wide range of Pokemon themed products including exclusive items that are often only available at Pokemon Centers across Japan. Next, we head to the Umeda Sky Building, a prominent landmark and skyscraper located in the Umeda District. The escalator ride up to the observatory is an experience in itself. Once at the top, you'll be met with a panoramic view of the city. After a full day of exploring, we head back to Dotonbori which has now come to life with neon lights illuminating the iconic entertainment district. Dotonbori is known for its lively streets and restaurants. It's here you can find some of Osaka's famous street food offerings, including takoyaki, which are octopus balls made of flour and pieces of octopus. However, we enjoy getting a taiyaki, which is a fish-shaped cake that usually comes with red bean filling inside. But for something more filling, we decided to head to one of the best okonomiyaki shops in the area Ajinoya, a Bib Gourmand - awarded okonomiyaki restaurant. I highly recommend getting here early, as the place is quite popular and you'll often find a long queue outside the restaurant. We ordered the Ajinoya mix okonomiyaki with some yakisoba on the side. After dinner, we take a quick evening stroll around the Hozenji Yokocho, a unique and atmospheric alleyway lined with small bars and eateries. While exploring, we also come across Hozenji, a small hidden temple dedicated to one of the five guardians of Buddhism. On our second day in Osaka, it's another early morning as we make a long journey north to visit the famous Katsuo-ji temple. The trip involves a train ride and a bus and takes a good one and a half hour to reach our destination. Katsuo-ji, also known as the Temple of Winner's Luck. It's a famous temple known for its beautiful garden featuring a large pond filled with koi fish. It's a prime destination for viewing the autumn colors and many come to spectate the change of seasons while enjoying the temple facilities. The temple is also known for housing hundreds, if not thousands, of Daruma dolls all scattered around the temple grounds. The doll symbolizes perseverance and good luck, and you can even purchase one as a small souvenir to bring home with you. Just remember there's a small fortune note inside. We return to Osaka and make a lunch stop at Izakaya Toyo, famous for its flame-wielding owner. This standing Izakaya is nestled away in a residential area near Kyobashi Station. The highlight is obviously to watch the owner Toyo as he prepares his signature dish. There aren't many items on the menu, but I recommend getting a set menu of fresh sashimi and of course the flame broiled tuna cheek. After a nice lunch, head over to Osaka Castle A historic and iconic landmark. It stands as a symbol of Osaka's rich history and cultural heritage. Take your time to explore the five-story castle and the surrounding castle grounds. As evening arrives, head to Shinsekai, a neon-lit retro district. The name Shinsekai translates to "the new world". The area was developed in the early 20th century to be a futuristic entertainment district. While the area has had its fair share of challenges over the years, it has retained its distinctive charm. Now it's home to many restaurants, quirky shops, retro arcades and most famously the Tsutenkaku tower. Our final agenda for today is to pay a visit to the TeamLab Botanical Garden. This permanent outdoor exhibition is only open during the evening. When night falls, the garden comes to life with bright colors and sounds, immersing you in a surreal but mesmerizing experience. You can easily spend more than two hours exploring the different displays. If you're interested in visiting, make sure to book your tickets a few days beforehand as they tend to get sold out. On our third day in Osaka, start the day by visiting the most iconic market in the city, Kuromon Market. Kuromon's history dates back to the Edo period and has become a popular destination for locals and tourists alike. The market is often referred to as Osaka's kitchen due to its wide range of fresh produce, seafood and street food. I decided to sample some sushi, so we found this small store, right in the market and got some pieces of tuna After Kuromon, we head south to Nipponbashi, also known as Den Den Town. It's known as the city's electronics and otaku culture hub. This area is similar to Tokyo's Akihabara and is a haven for anyone who's into anime, manga, gaming or electronics. You can find several stores dedicated to anime collectibles such as Kotobukiya and the popular Animate store. There's also arcades in the area where you can try your luck with the different machines or pass the time with some video games. After Den Den Town, we head west and visit the Kaiyukan one of Japan's most famous and impressive aquariums. The aquarium is located in the Tempozan Harbor Village along Osaka's waterfront. It's known for housing one of the largest marine tanks in the world and it's a great place for families to explore with its many themed zones and varied marine life. After finishing with the aquarium, you can head next door and take a short ride on the Tempozan Ferris Wheel as it lights up during the evening. It gives an excellent view of the Osaka Bay and the surrounding area. On our final day in Osaka, we decide to dedicate the day to Universal Studios Japan. The theme park is mostly known for its wild rides and themed areas. Specifically the Wizarding World of Harry Potter Minion Park Jurassic Park and the latest Super Nintendo World. If your aim is not to try every ride and you're just here for the atmosphere, then go with the 1 day studio pass. However, if your aim is to visit every area of the park and try every ride, then the express pass is an absolute must. As some areas such as the Super Nintendo World requires an area-timed ticket to enter, which is included with the express pass. In addition, you can significantly reduce the waiting times for rides. If you decide to visit, here are some basic tips. Buy your tickets in advance and make sure to download the USJ app to gain access to many of the park's features, such as being able to see wait times for rides and reserving area-timed tickets. Show up at least one hour or earlier before the official opening hours. And if you didn't purchase an express pass, then make sure to get the area-timed tickets either via the app or the physical ticket machines inside the park. And finally, avoid the weekends if possible. After Universal Studios, we make a final trip back to Dotonbori to grab some dinner and do some last minute shopping. We start off by visiting the Donguri store. The official Studio Ghibli store in Namba. A must visit if you're a Studio Ghibli fan, as you can get a picture with No-Face and Totoro while browsing the store's many goods. You may also consider visiting the large Don Quijote store in Dotonbori. The store is filled with all sorts of items and makes it the perfect spot for some last minute souvenirs. After all that shopping, take a ride on its famous Ferris wheel for a last bit of amusement. For dinner, I recommend swinging by Yakiniku M located in Hozenji Yokocho. They serve delicious cuts of meat and it's the perfect way to end the trip. If you're looking for a couple of extra things to do or just alternatives, here's a short list. Shitennoji and Sumiyasha Taisha, if you're looking for more temples or shrines to visit. There's also Abeno Harukas, the tallest building in Japan, and finally the Osaka Expo Park for a little side trip out of the city. And that's it for this 4 days guide to Osaka. If you intend to follow this itinerary, then it will run you about $938 US dollars. You can always find cheaper or more expensive accommodations depending on your budget, as well as food costs will vary depending on the type of restaurants you intend to visit. But this should give you a good idea of the expenses involved in such a trip. Alright, so hopefully that helps with the planning of your trip to Osaka. If you have any questions, leave them in the comments below. More info and links can be found in the description box, so do check that out. My name is Allan, thank you for watching this video, and I'll see you guys in the next one.

safetyNotes: And finally, avoid the weekends if possible

It's a relatively new hotel that comes with modern and clean rooms with all the usual amenities but the best part about the hotel is its location
There are many more nearby hotels you can stay at, but I'll leave links to hotel recommendations and more in the description box below
It's easy to keep track of your travel expenses and I found that using the digital card the most convenient as I could top up the card straight from the phone using a credit card
It may also be worth considering the Osaka Amazing Pass
We then head over to Umeda and make a quick stop at the Pokemon Center
Once at the top, you'll be met with a panoramic view of the city
But for something more filling, we decided to head to one of the best okonomiyaki shops in the area Ajinoya, a Bib Gourmand - awarded okonomiyaki restaurant
I highly recommend getting here early, as the place is quite popular and you'll often find a long queue outside the restaurant
We return to Osaka and make a lunch stop at Izakaya Toyo, famous for its flame-wielding owner
There aren't many items on the menu, but I recommend getting a set menu of fresh sashimi and of course the flame broiled tuna cheek
A must visit if you're a Studio Ghibli fan, as you can get a picture with No-Face and Totoro while browsing the store's many goods
For dinner, I recommend swinging by Yakiniku M located in Hozenji Yokocho

videos.transcript: What's up, guys? We're here in Osaka, and in this video, we're going to show you our top things to do here. So, hope you enjoy it. Nestled in the heart of Honshu, Osaka is Japan's third largest city and an important commercial center. As a true economic powerhouse, this sprawling metropolis is a forest of futuristic skyscrapers and high-speed energy. Often called the nation's kitchen, Osaka is also known for its street food and vibrant nightlife. In this video, we'll count down 10 of the best things to do in and around the city for your first time visit. At the end, we'll also share some must-know travel tips. Now, let's dive in. We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Located in Osaka's vibrant center, Dotonbori is one of Japan's most famous entertainment streets. By evening, this canal district transforms into a sensory explosion of neon signs, massive billboards, and bustling crowds. The area is connected by several small bridges, and from the Ebisubashi Bridge, you can walk directly into a huge covered shopping arcade packed with endless boutiques and cafés. To escape the heavy foot traffic while still taking in the neon reflections, hop on a 20-minute river cruise. It's a much more relaxing way to see the area, at least in our opinion. The city is home to hundreds of unique temples and shrines. Among these, Shitennoji Temple is a must visit as it's one of Japan's oldest Buddhist temples. It was founded in 593 by Prince Shotoku. The outer temple grounds are free to enter, but admission to the inner area and the garden is paid. Within the courtyard of the inner zone, you can find an impressive five-storied Pagoda as well as the Main Hall. This is where the Prince is enshrined as a statue. In contrast, the Namba Yasaka Shrine is famous for its lion hall, which is a massive building shaped like a roaring lion's head. It's believed that the lion's gaping mouth swallows evil spirits, leaving visitors with good luck. Entry to this shrine is free of charge. Situated in the center of the city, Osaka Castle is a monumental symbol of Japan's history. The current castle is a reconstruction surrounded by large stone walls and a double moat and was originally built by Toyotomi Hideyoshi. Inside, you'll find a modern museum detailing the castle's turbulent past. It's also topped with an observation deck that offers panoramic city views. You can easily reach the castle via different metro stations that surround its vast perimeter. The castle itself can get pretty busy at times, so if you're looking for some peace and quiet, you should definitely check out the surrounding park. It also has really good views of the castle walls from the outside. Located in the bustling Namba district, Kuromon Market is a lively covered arcade stretching 600 meters. This historic market features over 150 stalls packed with everything from fresh sushi and grilled seafood to high-quality wagyu beef and traditional sweets. The market also has many sit-down establishments where you can enjoy a meal away from the busy aisles. We took a break from the hustle and bustle at one of these local shops for a bowl of authentic udon. The market is generally open from 9 AM to 6 PM, but to avoid the heaviest crowds, try to visit outside the peak lunch rush. The skyline of Osaka is a sprawling forest of modern skyscrapers. For the ultimate bird's eye view, we visited Abeno Harukas, currently the tallest building in the city. Standing at 300 meter or 985 feet tall, the Harukas 300 observatory offers a stunning panorama of the entire Kansai region, including views of the distant Kyoto, Osaka Bay, and the Rokko Mountains. There's also an open-air deck space and a café where you can enjoy food and drinks. Additionally, the same building has a lower observation deck on the 16th floor garden terrace. This is a nice free option that still has some gorgeous views. Osaka is Japan's ultimate culinary capital, so eating your way through the city is a must. We joined a hands-on cooking class to master some of the city's most iconic soul foods: takoyaki and okonomiyaki. We learned to create the famous octopus-filled dough balls and layer the savory cabbage pancakes on a traditional griddle. The icing on the cake was enjoying our creations paired with sake and beer. This 2-hour session was led by a multilingual instructor. If you aren't ready to cook, a guided food tour is another great way to sample a wide variety of dishes. You can find links to the class that we did and some other food tour options in the description below the video. Sitting 90 kilometers or 56 miles to the west of Osaka, Himeji is reachable in about 30 minutes by taking the Shinkansen bullet train from Shin-Osaka Station. Alternatively, it takes 1 hour with the JR Special Rapid Train. It's a great day trip option since its main landmarks are located within a 20-minute walk from the station. The crown jewel is Himeji Castle, a UNESCO World Heritage Site and Japan's most iconic original castle. Known for its white plastered walls, this 17th century masterpiece was our absolute favorite in the country due to its sheer scale and beautiful preservation. Directly next door to the castle is Koko-en, a beautiful complex consisting of nine separate walled gardens designed in various Edo-period styles. Strolling through its tranquil koi ponds and bamboo groves was definitely a highlight for us. For the best perspective of the castle, make sure to take in the panoramas from one of the surrounding hills. Of the different parks around the castle, this one has the best view. Located along the waterfront, Universal Studios Japan is easily accessible by taking a 15-minute train ride from Osaka Station. This place features immersive lands ranging from the whimsical Minion Park to the classic Jurassic Park. It also includes the popular Super Nintendo World where you can step inside a life-sized video game. But our favorite was the wizarding world of Harry Potter, encompassing various themed rides and the snow-capped village of Hogsmeade. We loved the crooked chimneys, magical shops, and vast amounts of butterbeer. To maximize your time in the park, try to visit on a weekday when local crowds are thinner. Even better, invest in an express pass, which gives you quick access to specific popular attractions. Check out the links in the description below this video for e options. Located across Abeno Harukas in the southern part of the city, Tennoji Park is a sprawling and green space. It offers something for everyone from the lush Keitakuen Garden to the prominent Osaka City Museum of Fine Arts. We really enjoyed watching the sunset from the Wake Bridge where the sky lights up above the park's tranquil pond. For a lively atmosphere, head to the Tenshiba area, which features the iconic city sign as well as a variety of stylish bars and terrace restaurants. It's the perfect spot to transition from a relaxing stroll to an evening of local dining. Entry to the main area of the park is free, but the museum, zoo, and Keitakuen Garden require separate admission fees. Shinsekai is adjacent to Tennoji Park and is a nostalgic district that captures the city's old-world charm of the 20th century. Developed in 1912 with layouts inspired by Paris and New York, the area is centered around Tsutenkaku Tower. You can wander through the neon streets filled with restaurants, bars, and souvenir shops. Beyond dining, you can play vintage games or get a view from the tower's observation deck. We especially liked visiting during the evening, when the dazzling billboards and lit-up lanterns gave this area an electric energy. As we promised at the start of the video, we'll now share three essential travel tips for Osaka. For the best experience, stay somewhere around Namba, Shinsaibashi, or Hommachi. These are the most exciting dining, entertainment, and nightlife zones. We personally stayed in Hommachi and really enjoyed that area. Alternatively, stay in Kita. This is the city's main transport hub and business district. It offers the best connectivity for day trips to Himeji or Kyoto. Regardless of the neighborhood, try to book a hotel within walking distance of the Midosuji Subway Line as it connects almost all major tourist districts. The most efficient way to navigate the city is by using the Osaka Metro and the JR Osaka Loop Line. We used our Pasmo card from Tokyo to easily tap in and out of stations. You can even buy your transportation pass directly from your phone's digital wallet, allowing you to top up using your credit card. We also definitely recommend using Google Maps since it provides specific navigation, including exact platform numbers, car boarding suggestions, and station exit codes. Osaka is served by three major airports, and it's easy to mix them up when booking domestic flights. Kansai is the massive hub used for most international and budget flights, and it's built on a man-made island. Itami is much closer to the city center, but serves only domestic routes. However, some domestic flights also depart from Kobe airport, which is about an hour away from central Osaka. Of course, there's a few more things to do in and around the city that we didn't mention, so we put a short list on the screen here for you in case you have more time or are looking for other options. Thanks for watching, and we hope you enjoyed it. You can find more videos on our channel about Japan as well. These include other destinations and more tips for exploring the country. And if you're planning your own trip to Japan, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below the video. We included all the important links you'll need for your Osaka experience in the description as well. We'll catch you on the next adventure.

safetyNotes: The market is generally open from 9 AM to 6 PM, but to avoid the heaviest crowds, try to visit outside the peak lunch rush

videos.transcript: Hi, I’m Toshi from Japan and tourist guide for foreigners. In this video, I will show you 6 Best Areas to stay in Osaka. I've gotten a lot of questions from viewers about the best places to stay in Osaka. But as Japan’s second-largest city, with a population of 2.7 million, the best place really depends on what you’re looking for. Maybe you want a prime location, an affordable spot, or to be near a bustling nightlife. With over 30 years living in Japan and guiding countless international visitors, I know what makes each area of Osaka unique. In this video, I'll cover six top areas for accommodation, highlighting their key features, who they’re best for, and how easy they are to reach. Plus, I'll share my top hotel recommendations and booking tips for each area, and even which spots you might want to avoid. By the end, you'll know how to have a more comfortable, efficient, and enjoyable trip to Osaka. So be sure to stick around till the end! So, let’s get started. What is Osaka? Before diving into Osaka’s best areas to stay, let’s first understand what Osaka is all about. It’s Japan's second-largest city, home to around 2.7 million people, and a major hub of activity. Positioned along Osaka Bay, this bustling port city has been a gateway for international trade for centuries. Today, it blends tradition with modernity, offering travelers a rich mix of attractions. Osaka is large and diverse, with each area offering its own unique vibe. Namba, for example, is famous for its bright neon Glico sign and lively atmosphere. Tennoji is known for its iconic Tsutenkaku Tower and vibrant shopping streets, while Osaka Castle, one of Japan’s top three castles, dominates its namesake area. If you’re staying in Osaka for more than five days, I recommend splitting your stay between two different areas. It’s a great way to fully experience the diverse character of the city. And for more in-depth guides on Osaka’s attractions, be sure to check out my previous video. **Where to stay** **Top6: Osaka Castle Area** 6. Why is the Osaka Castle Area? Osaka Castle is an iconic landmark you can’t miss when visiting Osaka. The grand scale of the castle, especially the breathtaking view from the top of the main tower, is awe-inspiring. Surrounding the castle is a spacious park that locals love for jogging and walking their dogs—it's a perfect relaxation spot for residents. But it’s not just about nature; the area is also surrounded by office buildings, with plenty of restaurants and izakayas nearby. Additionally, you’ll find Tsuruhashi Korea Town, Japan’s largest Korea Town, a vibrant spot popular with tourists for experiencing a unique blend of Korean and Japanese culture. 6.What about the location in the Osaka Castle Area? Getting around here is easy, as six train lines serve the Osaka Castle area. While Morinomiya and Osaka-Castle Park stations are the closest, the park is so vast that you can use many other nearby stations depending on where you are. 6.Which hotel in the Osaka Castle Area? For a great stay, I recommend Hotel New Otani Osaka. This upscale hotel is right next to Osaka Castle Park, offering rooms with stunning views of Osaka Castle. If you’re looking for an area that combines history, nature, and lively nightlife, the Osaka Castle area is the perfect choice. **Top5: Shin-Osaka Area** 5. Why is the Shin-Osaka Area? Shin-Osaka is ideal if you prioritize convenience. It’s home to Shin-Osaka Station, where Shinkansen depart. You can easily reach far-off places like Tokyo, Yokohama, or Hiroshima without transfers. If you need to catch an early morning Shinkansen, staying here is perfect. It’s also a great base for day trips to nearby cities like Kyoto, Nara, and Kobe. Plus, it’s popular with business travelers, so there are many budget-friendly, single-occupancy hotel options. While the area lacks tourist spots, it’s very quiet at night when the trains stop running. 5. What about the location in the Shin-Osaka Area? As mentioned, it’s well-connected with both Shinkansen and local lines, making it easy to reach distant or nearby attractions. You can even travel directly to Osaka’s main tourist hubs like Namba, Umeda, and Tennoji without changing trains. 5. Which hotel in the Shin-Osaka Area? A great choice is the Shin-Osaka Station Hotel, just a 4-minute walk from the station. It’s also a treat for kids who love trains, as they can watch the Shinkansen up close. This area is perfect for those focused on convenience and early-morning travel plans. **Top4: USJ Area** 4. Why is the USJ Area? This is perfect for those who want to fully enjoy Universal Studios Japan, located on Osaka's western side. The area is home to USJ and offers plenty of nearby restaurants and shopping options. Just keep in mind that many shops close when the theme park does, so there aren't many late-night options. You can also reach Osaka's famous aquarium, Kaiyukan, in 15 minutes by ferry. Plus, the 2025 Osaka Expo will be hosted in this area starting in April 2025. 4. What about the location in the USJ Area? It’s just 15 minutes from Umeda and 30 minutes from Namba, making it a well-situated spot. There’s also a direct bus from Kansai International Airport, ideal for those heading straight to USJ after landing. If you’re planning to visit USJ, Kaiyukan, and the Osaka Expo, this area is a great choice. 4. Which hotel in the USJ Area? I recommend staying at Kintetsu Universal City. It offers a range of rooms, from standard to themed rooms featuring USJ's Sesame Street characters. It’s especially popular with couples and families. If you want to enjoy USJ from open to close without worrying about transportation, this area is ideal for you. **Top3: Tennoji Area** 3. Why is the Tennoji Area? Tennoji is a diverse area that combines shopping, nature, and a nostalgic atmosphere. One highlight is Abeno Harukas, Japan’s tallest commercial building at 300 meters, offering incredible views of the Kansai region from its observation deck. A 10-minute walk away, you'll find Tennoji Park, a peaceful green space perfect for a picnic, even amidst the bustling city. The park also has a popular zoo, making it great for families. A 15-minute walk from there brings you to the Shinsekai area, home to the iconic Tsutenkaku Tower and a lively, retro shopping street. This area has an old-school vibe, where you can enjoy traditional Japanese games and experiences. 3. What about the location in the Tennoji Area? Tennoji is just a 5-minute train ride from Osaka’s main hub, Namba, making it super accessible. Plus, all the attractions I just mentioned are within walking distance. It’s also conveniently close to Nara, another popular destination from Osaka. 3. Which hotel in the Tennoji Area? I recommend Hotel Bali Tower Osaka Tennoji. It’s a resort-style hotel that offers a relaxing, tropical atmosphere at a reasonable price, right in the middle of Osaka’s urban scene. For those who want to experience shopping, nature, and a bit of old Osaka, Tennoji is a fantastic choice. **Top2: Umeda Area** 2. Why is the Umeda Area? Umeda area is a major hub in Osaka, featuring the city’s largest terminal station, making it super convenient for travelers. You’ll find plenty of department stores and big retail shops here. Plus, it’s just a 5-minute train ride from Shin-Osaka, perfect if you’re catching a bullet train to cities like Tokyo or Hiroshima. The area is known for its vibrant nightlife, with restaurants and bars open late, and trains running past midnight. While it’s more of a business district with fewer tourist attractions, it has a wide range of hotels to choose from, making it easy to find something that suits your style and budget. 2. What about the location in the Umeda Area? Umeda is very well-connected, with 7 train lines passing through. It’s also a great jumping-off point for nearby spots like Kyoto, Nara, and Kobe, making it ideal if you want to explore beyond Osaka. 2. Which hotel in the Umeda Area? One hotel recommendation here is the Hearton Hotel Nishi Umeda. It’s a great value in an area known for more upscale options, offering convenience without breaking the bank. Overall, Umeda is perfect if you’re looking for a mix of shopping, dining, and easy access to transportation. **Top1: Namba Area** 1. Why is the Namba Area? Namba is Osaka’s most vibrant downtown, known for its iconic Glico sign and flashy billboards. It's a hotspot where visitors often strike a pose with the famous Glico Man for photos. The main street is lined with countless eateries, offering delicious food year-round. You can also enjoy riverside walks and boat cruises. Nearby, you'll find attractions like Kuromon Market, famous for fresh seafood, and the nostalgic Hozenji Yokocho alley. Namba captures Osaka’s lively spirit, offering a mix of culture, energy, and local flavor, making it a must-visit for anyone wanting to experience the heart of the city. 1. What about the location in the Namba Area? It's well-connected: just 20 minutes from Umeda and an hour directly from Kansai Airport, making it ideal for international travelers arriving in Osaka. 1. Which hotel in the Namba Area? I recommend Natural Hot Spring Onyado Nono Namba, which offers a cozy ryokan-like stay with a natural hot spring, right in the middle of all the action. However, Namba has a wide range of lodging options, from luxury hotels to budget guesthouses, so you can find the perfect fit for your travel style. If you're looking for nightlife, location and great experiences, Namba is the ideal choice. **Where not to stay** Why Not Stay in the Nishinari Area? Nishinari has a reputation as one of the rougher areas in Osaka, with a significant homeless population and a history of safety concerns. Many locals advise caution here. But in recent years, police presence has increased, making it safer and more appealing. The biggest draw is affordability—you can find private rooms for under 2,000 yen and drinks from vending machines at half the usual price. It's also conveniently located as part of the Tennoji area, and some foreign tourists call this area "cool Nishinari." It's easy on the wallet and is quickly becoming a popular tourist destination. However, compared to other areas, the security situation is not good, so I recommend that women and children in particular avoid this area. **How We Book Hotel** Hotel Booking Tip Let’s talk about some important tips for booking hotels in Japan, as well as my recommended platforms. First, if you already have your travel schedule, always book your hotel before you arrive. As I’ve mentioned in previous videos, Japan is experiencing overtourism, and this has led to a shortage of hotel staff and accommodation options. This means demand often exceeds supply, and if you don’t book ahead, you may struggle to find a place to stay upon arrival. Even if you do find a room, it might not be in a convenient location, or you could end up paying much more than you expected. I had a friend from the U.S. who didn’t book in advance, and since he arrived on a weekend, he ended up staying at an overpriced hotel in a bad location. He really regretted not planning ahead. To avoid this, I highly recommend booking at least the first few days of your stay before you leave for Japan. Which Platforms to Use? When it comes to booking hotels in Japan, I recommend using either Expedia or Booking.com. I’ve traveled to over 75 countries, and these are my go-to platforms, both internationally and in Japan. They offer a smooth experience and a wide selection of accommodations. If you’re using Expedia, consider using the link in the description to support my channel at no extra cost to you. Your support helps me create more content like this. So if you're booking through Expedia, I’d really appreciate it! How was today’s video? If you would like to request a guide in Japan or have any questions regarding your travel itinerary, please contact me from the link in the description section. Don’t forget to subscribe to my channel and like button. See you in my next video, bye!
